title: Regular Expression DoS (ReDoS)
summary: An input validator uses a regex with catastrophic backtracking. Five stages hide the flaw in different patterns — a nested quantifier, an email regex, an overlapping alternation, a too-high length cap, and a character-class nested quantifier — until a linear pattern with an input bound.
goal: Send a short input that makes the validation regex run for a long time (catastrophic backtracking).
goalSecure: Send the same payloads to the fixed stage — validation stays fast because the pattern is linear.
rootCause: The validation regex contains a nested quantifier or overlapping alternation, so certain inputs force the backtracking engine to explore exponentially many ways to match before failing.
lessons: A quantifier inside another quantifier (a+)+ backtracks exponentially on a non-matching suffix.
Real-world regexes (emails, URLs) often smuggle in nested quantifiers.
Alternations whose branches overlap cause exponential backtracking.
A length cap isn't a ReDoS defense unless it's tiny — blowup happens at a few dozen chars.
Character-class nested quantifiers ((\w+\s*)*) are just as exponential as (a+)+.
Use linear patterns (no nested quantifiers / ambiguous alternation), bound input, or a linear engine (RE2).

stages.title: Nested quantifier in an email regex
Length cap that’s too high
stages.defense: A nested-quantifier regex, but inputs are capped at 40 chars.

stages.title: Linear regex + input bound
stages.defense: A non-backtracking pattern with a small length bound.
